data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The Sea Peoples were not a primary cause but a symptom of systemic collapse triggered by climatic anomaly","mechanism": "A prolonged regional drought (c. 1250-1150 BC) caused crop failures, famine, and social unrest that simultaneously destabilized palace economies dependent on surplus extraction; the Sea Peoples were displaced populations fleeing ecological stress, whose invasions were opportunistic rather than causally primary","scope": {"covers": ["Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Mycenaean Greece", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Assyria"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Egyptian agricultural records show no drought stress during 1250-1150 BC and the Nile flood levels remained stable", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Palace economies showed continued surplus storage capacity without administrative contraction before destruction layers", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "The Sea Peoples are attested as organized polities with territorial ambitions in contemporary texts predating the collapses", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Candidate foregrounds environmental determinism; Egypt excluded because Nile hydrology buffered against rainfall-dependent agriculture. Typicality reflects mainstream but contested climate-catastrophe narrative."}
